10.1016/j.jmp.2016.09.001
In this paper, we search for characterizations of the continuous representability of biorders. Besides, we use the concept of biorder in order to mathematically formalize the order structure of a distributed system.

The concept of a biorder was introduced by André Ducamp and Jean-Claude Falmagne in 1969 in  Ducamp and Falmagne (1969) , and studied in depth in 1984 by Jean-Paul Doignon, André Ducamp and Jean-Claude Falmagne in  Doignon, Ducamp, and Falmagne (1984) . It is defined as follows:

A biorder  
                      
                         R 
                       
                      from  
                      
                         A 
                       
                      to  
                      
                         X 
                       
                      is a binary relation, with  
                      
                         R 
                         ⊆ 
                         A 
                         × 
                         X 
                      
                      , satisfying that for every  
                      
                         a 
                         , 
                         b 
                         ∈ 
                         A 
                       
                      and  
                      
                         x 
                         , 
                         y 
                         ∈ 
                         X 
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            R 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         ∧ 
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            R 
                            y 
                            ) 
                         
                       
                      implies  
                      
                         
                            ( 
                            a 
                            R 
                            y 
                            ) 
                         
                         ∨ 
                         
                            ( 
                            b 
                            R 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      
                      .

Although we will work with biorders between two sets  
                         A 
                       and  
                         X 
                      , in the literature ( Aleskerov, Bouyssou, & Monjardet, 2007; Doignon et al., 1984 ) the concept of biorder can also be found just as a Ferrers relation, that is, as a relation  
                         R 
                       on a single set  
                         X 
                       such that for any  
                         x 
                         , 
                         y 
                         , 
                         z 
                         , 
                         t 
                         ∈ 
                         X 
                       it holds that  
                         x 
                         R 
                         y 
                       with  
                         z 
                         R 
                         t 
                       implies that  
                         x 
                         R 
                         t 
                       or  
                         z 
                         R 
                         y 
                      . Anyway, notice that if  
                         R 
                       is a relation from  
                         A 
                       to  
                         X 
                      , then it is also a relation from  
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               ′ 
                            
                         
                       to  
                         
                            
                               X 
                            
                            
                               ′ 
                            
                         
                      , for any  
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               ′ 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         
                            
                               X 
                            
                            
                               ′ 
                            
                         
                       such that  
                         A 
                         ⊆ 
                         
                            
                               A 
                            
                            
                               ′ 
                            
                         
                       and  
                         X 
                         ⊆ 
                         
                            
                               X 
                            
                            
                               ′ 
                            
                         
                      . Therefore,  
                         R 
                       is also a relation on the single set  
                         A 
                         ∪ 
                         X 
                       ( Doignon et al., 1984 ).

Thus, the concept of biorder includes the well-known concept of interval order (and consequently, the more restrictive one of semiorder) ( Aleskerov et al., 2007; Bosi, Candeal, & Induráin, 2007; Bridges & Mehta, 1995; Fishburn, 1970; Luce, 1956; Scott & Suppes, 1958; Tversky, 1969 ), that actually is just an irreflexive biorder on a single set  
                         X 
                       ( Aleskerov et al., 2007; Doignon et al., 1984 ).

The motivations to study  continuous  representability are various. We should observe that a numerical representation of a biorder translates the  qualitative  scale defined by the given biorder into a  quantitative  one, namely the real line with its usual order. Of course, it is much easier to compare numbers than to compare objects or elements belonging to an abstract set in a (just) qualitative way. Moreover, it is quite common to encounter scales in our word that vary in a continuous manner, so that the quantification that corresponds to the search for a suitable numerical representation should also be made in a way that preserves continuity.

For the particular case of an interval order several results on continuous representability appeared in the last years ( Bosi, Campión, Candeal, & Induráin, 2007; Bosi, Estevan, Gutiérrez García & Induráin, 2015; Estevan, Gutiérrez García, & Induráin, 2013a ). However, there is a certain lack of results on continuous representability of biorders (though partial results on connected topological spaces are well-known  Chateauneuf, 1987 ) and up-to-date  no general characterization of the continuous representability of a biorder is known yet .

Here we introduce a new set of necessary conditions for the continuous representability of biorders. Special attention is paid to biorders on finite sets (and also to those biorders defined on sets whose quotients are finite with respect to the traces), where we prove that those necessary conditions for the continuous representability are also sufficient. We also focus on biorders defined between totally preordered sets (see  Fig. 1 
                      ), where a characterization of the continuous representability of the order structure is achieved too.

The study of these order structures, pairs of biordered and disjoint sets (that can be generalized to  
                         n 
                       disjoint sets  
                         
                            
                               X 
                            
                            
                               1 
                            
                         
                         , 
                         … 
                         , 
                         
                            
                               X 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                       linked by  
                         n 
                         
                            ( 
                            n 
                            − 
                            1 
                            ) 
                         
                       biorders defined from  
                         
                            
                               X 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                       to  
                         
                            
                               X 
                            
                            
                               j 
                            
                         
                      , for any  
                         i 
                         ≠ 
                         j 
                      ), is also interesting dealing with distributed systems ( Fidge, 1991; Lamport, 1978; Mattern, 0000; Raynal & Singhal, 1991 ). An  event  (illustrated by a point in  Figs. 1 and 2 
                      ) is a uniquely identified runtime instance of an atomic action of interest. It is an occurrence at a point in time, i.e., a happening at a cut of the timeline, which itself does not take any time. A  process  (illustrated by a vertical line in  Figs. 1 and 2 ) is a sequence of a totally ordered events, i.e., for any event  
                         a 
                       and  
                         b 
                       in a process, either  
                         a 
                       comes before  
                         b 
                       or  
                         b 
                       comes before  
                         a 
                      . A  distributed system  consists of a collection of distinct processes which are spatially separated (so, disjoint from a mathematical point of view), and which communicate with one another by exchanging messages: this communication (illustrated by red arrows in  Fig. 1  and wavy arrows in  Fig. 2 ) implies an order between events of different processes. It is assumed that sending or receiving a message is an event.

Since each process consists of a sequence of events, each process is a totally ordered set (we will generalize it to totally preordered sets), and the communication through messages between the processes can be mathematically formalized by means of biorders.

Moreover, this communication between processes defines a causal relation known as ‘causal precedence’ or ‘happened before’ relation ( Fidge, 1991; Lamport, 1978; Mattern, 0000; Raynal & Singhal, 1991 ) (common in causality too  Panangaden, 2014 , but now related to the theory of relativity, see also  Busemann, 1967 ,  Kronheimer & Penrose, 1966 ), which is a strict partial order on the elements of the sets. This causal relation is a powerful concept for reasoning, analysing and drawing inferences about distributed computation ( Raynal & Singhal, 1991 ). The  causal precedence  (denoted by  
                         → 
                      ) on the set of events of a system is defined by Leslie Lamport ( Lamport, 1978 ) as the smallest relation satisfying the following three conditions:  
                         
                            1. 
                            If  
                                  a 
                                and  
                                  b 
                                are events in the same process, and  
                                  a 
                                comes before  
                                  b 
                               , then  
                                  a 
                                  → 
                                  b 
                               . 
                         
                         
                            2. 
                            If  
                                  a 
                                is the sending of a message by one process and  
                                  b 
                                is the receipt of the same message by another process, then  
                                  a 
                                  → 
                                  b 
                               . 
                         
                         
                            3. 
                            If  
                                  a 
                                  → 
                                  b 
                                and  
                                  b 
                                  → 
                                  c 
                                then  
                                  a 
                                  → 
                                  c 
                               .

If  
                                  a 
                                and  
                                  b 
                                are events in the same process, and  
                                  a 
                                comes before  
                                  b 
                               , then  
                                  a 
                                  → 
                                  b 
                               .

If  
                                  a 
                                is the sending of a message by one process and  
                                  b 
                                is the receipt of the same message by another process, then  
                                  a 
                                  → 
                                  b 
                               .

If  
                                  a 
                                  → 
                                  b 
                                and  
                                  b 
                                  → 
                                  c 
                                then  
                                  a 
                                  → 
                                  c 
                               .

This definition was introduced by Leslie Lamport in 1978 (see  Lamport, 1978 ) and it has been used until nowadays. In the present paper, we shall introduce a mathematical definition of the concept through orderings.

Graphically,  
                         α 
                         → 
                         β 
                       means that one can follow a ‘path of causality’ from event  
                         α 
                       to event  
                         β 
                       in the diagram, moving in the direction of the arrows (see  Fig. 1  or  Fig. 2 ).

Two distinct events  
                         a 
                       and  
                         b 
                       are concurrent if  
                         a 
                         ↛ 
                         b 
                       and  
                         b 
                         ↛ 
                         a 
                      . Assume  
                         a 
                         ↛ 
                         a 
                       for any event  
                         a 
                      , so  
                         → 
                       is an irreflexive partial ordering on the set of all events in the system. The ordering is only partial because events can be concurrent in which case it is not known which event happened first. Two events  
                         a 
                       and  
                         b 
                       are concurrent if they in no way can causally affect each other, that is, there is no causality between them.

Given a preorder  
                         ≾ 
                       on  
                         X 
                      , a real function  
                         u 
                         : 
                         X 
                         → 
                         R 
                       is said to be  isotonic  or  increasing  if for every  
                         x 
                         , 
                         y 
                         ∈ 
                         X 
                       the implication  
                         x 
                         ≾ 
                         y 
                         ⇒ 
                         u 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         ≤ 
                         u 
                         
                            ( 
                            y 
                            ) 
                         
                       holds true. In the case of a total preorder  
                         ≾ 
                       on  
                         X 
                      , it is said to be  representable  if there is a real-valued function  
                         u 
                         : 
                         X 
                         → 
                         R 
                       that is  strictly isotonic  or  strictly increasing  (also known as  order-preserving ), so that, for every  
                         x 
                         , 
                         y 
                         ∈ 
                         X 
                      , it holds that  
                         x 
                         ≾ 
                         y 
                         
                         ⟺ 
                         
                         u 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         ≤ 
                         u 
                         
                            ( 
                            y 
                            ) 
                         
                      . The map  
                         u 
                       is said to be an  order-monomorphism  (also known as a  utility function  for  
                         ≾ 
                      ).

A (not necessarily total) preorder  
                         ≾ 
                       on a set  
                         X 
                       is said to have a  multi-utility representation  if there exists a family  
                         U 
                       of isotonic real functions such that for all points  
                         x 
                         , 
                         y 
                         ∈ 
                         X 
                       the equivalence  
                         x 
                         ≾ 
                         y 
                         ⇔ 
                         ∀ 
                         u 
                         ∈ 
                         U 
                         
                         u 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         ≤ 
                         u 
                         
                            ( 
                            y 
                            ) 
                         
                       holds. This kind of representation, whose main feature is to fully characterize the preorder, was first introduced by  Levin (1985)  (see also  Levin, 2001 ), who called  functionally closed  a preorder admitting a multi-utility representation. However, the first systematic study of multi-utility representations is due to  Evren and Ok (2011) , who presented different conditions for the existence of continuous multi-utility representations. Although not all total preorders are representable by a utility function (see  Bridges & Mehta, 1995 ), a multi-utility representation always exists for every not necessarily total preorder  
                         ≾ 
                       on  
                         X 
                       (see Proposition 1 in  Evren & Ok, 2011 ). It is also interesting to search for a  continuous multi-utility representation  of a preorder  
                         ≾ 
                       when the set  
                         X 
                       is endowed with a topology  
                         τ 
                       (cf., for instance,  Alcantud, Bosi, & Zuanon, 2016 ;  Evren & Ok, 2011 ).

A particular case of the previous representation is the so called  Richter–Peleg multi-utility representation  (introduced by Ettore Minguzzi in  Minguzzi, 2013 ), which holds when all the functions of the multi-utility representation  
                         U 
                       are order-preserving for the preorder  
                         ≾ 
                       (i.e., for all  
                         u 
                         ∈ 
                         U 
                      , and  
                         x 
                         , 
                         y 
                         ∈ 
                         X 
                      ,  
                         x 
                         ≺ 
                         y 
                       implies that  
                         u 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                         < 
                         u 
                         
                            ( 
                            y 
                            ) 
                         
                      ).

The construction of the aforementioned representations for a preorder may be a hard problem. For this purpose, the study of the (continuous) representability of biorders defined between totally preordered sets seems a right approach in order to achieve a (continuous and finite) Richter–Peleg multi-utility representation of the corresponding causal precedence or happened before relation. This idea consists in using (continuous) representations of distributed systems in order to represent (continuously) the corresponding causal precedence relation (see  Proposition 40 ). As we said, the causal precedence is a preorder, but a preorder could fail to be the causal precedence of a distributed system. Therefore, dealing with this technique a main question arises: Which kind of preorders are actually a causal precedence of a distributed system? That is, which preorders can be interpreted as a causal precedence arising from biorders between some totally preordered sets?

In Section   4  we focus on a distributed system of two sets  
                         A 
                       and  
                         X 
                      , where there is a communication (that is, a biorder) from  
                         A 
                       to  
                         X 
                       but also from  
                         X 
                       to  
                         A 
                      . Here we characterize the continuity of the representation of this order structure as well as the representation of the corresponding preorder (the associated causal precedence or happened before relation) on  
                         A 
                         ∪ 
                         X 
                      . Therefore, this paper tries to be a possible first key for future studies on distributed systems, as well as on causal relations and preorders. For this purpose, a generalization of this order structure would be needed by means of ‘ 
                         n 
                      ’ totally preordered sets (processes) and biorders between each pair of sets (communication between processes).

The structure of the paper goes as follows : After the Introduction and Preliminaries, in Section   3  we introduce new results through some necessary conditions for the continuous representability of biorders. In Section   4 , we analyse the continuous representability of biorders defined from a totally preordered topological space  
                         
                            ( 
                            A 
                            , 
                            
                               
                                  τ 
                               
                               
                                  A 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       to another totally preordered topological space  
                         
                            ( 
                            X 
                            , 
                            τ 
                            ) 
                         
                      , with the additional condition that the functions also represent the corresponding total preorders. Here, we also include a mathematical definition for the concept of ‘distributed system’. In Section   5 , we solve the case in which the quotient sets–with respect to the indifference of the traces–are finite and, in particular, the case in which the sets are finite. A section of final remarks closes the paper.

From now on,

                       and  
                         X 
                       will denote nonempty sets. When we speak of continuity of a real-valued mapping defined on a set  
                         S 
                      , we assume that some topology

The  transitive closure  of a binary relation  
                               R 
                             on a set  
                               X 
                             is the transitive relation  
                               
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                                  
                                     + 
                                  
                               
                             on set  
                               X 
                             such that  
                               
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                                  
                                     + 
                                  
                               
                             contains  
                               R 
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     R 
                                  
                                  
                                     + 
                                  
                               
                             is minimal.

The  transitive reduction  of a binary relation  
                               R 
                             on a set  
                               X 
                             is the smallest relation having the transitive closure of  
                               R 
                             as its transitive closure.

In the particular case of a nonempty set where some kind of  ordering  (e.g., preorder, interval order, biorder, etc.) has been defined, the standard notation is different. We include it here for the sake of completeness, and we will use it throughout the present manuscript.  
                         Definition 2 
                         A  preorder  
                            
                               ≾ 
                             on  
                               X 
                             is a binary relation on  
                               X 
                             which is reflexive and transitive. An antisymmetric preorder is said to be an  order . A  total preorder  
                            
                               ≾ 
                             on a set  
                               X 
                             is a preorder such that if  
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                             then  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  x 
                                  ≾ 
                                  y 
                                  ] 
                               
                               ∨ 
                               
                                  [ 
                                  y 
                                  ≾ 
                                  x 
                                  ] 
                               
                            . A total order is also called a  linear order , and a totally ordered set  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  ≾ 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is also said to be a  chain . Usually, an order that fails to be total is also said to be a  partial order  and it is also denoted by  
                               ⪯ 
                            . 
                         If  
                               ≾ 
                             is a preorder on  
                               X 
                            , then as usual we denote the associated  asymmetric  relation or  strict preorder  by  
                               ≺ 
                             and the associated  equivalence  relation by  
                               ∼ 
                             and these are defined, respectively, by  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  x 
                                  ≺ 
                                  y 
                                  
                                  ⟺ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     ≾ 
                                     y 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ∧ 
                                  ¬ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     y 
                                     ≾ 
                                     x 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ] 
                               
                             and  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  x 
                                  ∼ 
                                  y 
                                  
                                  ⟺ 
                                  
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     ≾ 
                                     y 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ∧ 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     y 
                                     ≾ 
                                     x 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ] 
                               
                            . The asymmetric part of a linear order (respectively, of a total preorder) is said to be a  strict linear order  (respectively, a strict total preorder).

                               A

                             can be found in the literature ( Bouyssou & Marchant, 2011; Doignon et al., 1984; Nakamura, 2002 ).

Next  Definition 7  introduces the notion of representability 
                         1 
                      
                      
                         1 
                         The notion of representability is not unique (see  Aleskerov et al., 2007 ,  Doignon et al., 1984 ,  Estevan, Gutiérrez García, & Induráin, 2013b ,  Nakamura, 2002 ). 
                       for total preorders and biorders. The idea behind representability corresponds to the possibility of converting qualitative scales (preferences) into quantitative ones, comparing real numbers instead of, just, elements of a nonempty set. When we speak of continuity of a real-valued mapping defined on a set  
                         S 
                      , we assume that some topology  
                         
                            
                               τ 
                            
                            
                               S 
                            
                         
                       is given on  
                         S 
                      .

Definition 7 
                         A total preorder  
                               ≾ 
                             on  
                               X 
                             is called  representable  if there is a real-valued function  
                               u 
                               : 
                               X 
                               → 
                               R 
                             that is order-preserving, so that, for every  
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            , it holds that  
                               
                                  [ 
                                  x 
                                  ≾ 
                                  y 
                                  
                                  ⟺ 
                                  
                                  u 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     x 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ≤ 
                                  u 
                                  
                                     ( 
                                     y 
                                     ) 
                                  
                                  ] 
                               
                            . The map  
                               u 
                             is said to be an  order-monomorphism  (also known as a  utility function  for  
                               ≾ 
                            ). 
                         A biorder  
                               ≺ 
                             from  
                               A 
                             to  
                               X 
                             is said to be  representable  (as well as  realizable with respect to  
                            
                               < 
                            ) 
                               2 
                            
                            
                               2 
                               
                                  Realizability is also studied with respect to  
                                  
                                     ≤

                               ≾

                               X

                               ≾

Definition 8 
                         Let

                         These concepts of  Definition 8  can be characterized by means of nets as it was done in  Estevan et al. (2013a) .

Definition 10 
                         A biorder  
                               ≺ 
                             from  
                               A 
                             to  
                               X 
                             is said to be  continuously representable on  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  A 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        A 
                                     
                                  
                                  )

                                  u 
                                  , 
                                  v 
                                  )

                               u 
                               : 
                               A 
                               → 
                               R 
                             is continuous when  
                               A 
                             is given the topology  
                               
                                  
                                     τ 
                                  
                                  
                                     A 
                                  
                               
                             and the real line is given its usual topology. Dually,  
                               ≺ 
                             is said to be  continuously representable on  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        X 
                                     
                                  
                                  )

                                  u

                               v 
                               : 
                               X 
                               →

                               X 
                             is given the topology  
                               
                                  
                                     τ 
                                  
                                  
                                     X

                                  ( 
                                  u 
                                  , 
                                  v 
                                  ) 
                               
                             such that both functions  
                               u 
                             and  
                               v 
                             are continuous.

Now, we show an example of a biorder. In this example, we can see that it makes sense (because of the context, due to lack of topology in one of the sets,  
                         … 
                      ) to study representations  
                         
                            ( 
                            u 
                            , 
                            v 
                            ) 
                         
                       of a biorder such that just one of the functions ( 
                         u 
                       or  
                         v 
                      ) is continuous. If there is a lack of topology in one of the sets, we can endow it with the discrete topology.

                             is endowed with the discrete topology  
                               
                                  
                                     τ 
                                  
                                  
                                     X 
                                  
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     τ 
                                  
                                  
                                     D 
                                  
                               
                            , then it is easy to check that  
                               
                                  ( 
                                  ι 
                                  , 
                                  u 
                                  ) 
                               
                             is a continuous representation of the biorder.

Remarks 12 
                         The example above shows some important conclusions:  
                               
                                  (1) 
                                  There exist continuous representations of biorders such that the functions fail to represent the corresponding traces. In fact, notice too that the trace  
                                        
                                           
                                              ≾ 
                                           
                                           
                                              ∗ 
                                           
                                        
                                      fails to be  
                                        
                                           
                                              τ 
                                           
                                           
                                              A 
                                           
                                        
                                     -continuous. 
                               
                               
                                  (2) 
                                  Furthermore, the biorder of the example can only be continuously represented just if we renounce to represent the traces.

There exist continuous representations of biorders such that the functions fail to represent the corresponding traces. In fact, notice too that the trace

Furthermore, the biorder of the example can only be continuously represented just if we renounce to represent the traces.

Let us recall now some characterizations of the representability, for total preorders and biorders.

Definition 13 
                         A total preorder  
                               ≾ 
                             defined on  
                               X 
                             is said to be  perfectly separable  if there exists a countable subset  
                               D 
                               ⊆ 
                               X 
                             such that for every  
                               x 
                               , 
                               y 
                               ∈ 
                               X

                               x 
                               ≺ 
                               y 
                             there exists  
                               d 
                               ∈ 
                               D 
                             such that  
                               x 
                               ≾ 
                               d 
                               ≾ 
                               y 
                            .

                               ≺

                               A 
                             to  
                               X

                               M 
                             of  
                               A 
                               ∪ 
                               X 
                             is said to be  strictly dense  (see  Aleskerov et al., 2007 ,  Doignon et al., 1984 ) if for all  
                               a 
                               ∈ 
                               A 
                             and  
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               X

                               a 
                               ≺

                               m

                               a 
                               ≺

                               x

                               m

Theorem 15 
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               A 
                             
                            and  
                            
                               X 
                             
                            be two nonempty sets. 
                            
                               
                                  (a)

                                        ≾ 
                                      
                                     on  
                                     
                                        X 
                                      
                                     is representable if and only if it is perfectly separable.

                                        ≺

                                        A 
                                      
                                     to  
                                     
                                        X 
                                      
                                     is representable if and only if there exists a countable strictly dense subset.

Proof 
                         These results are well-known ( Bridges & Mehta, 1995; Doignon et al., 1984 ). □

These results are well-known ( Bridges & Mehta, 1995; Doignon et al., 1984 ). □

                               R

                       appears in  Estevan et al. (2013b) .

                         Corollary 17

Throughout this Section   3 , we retrieve the study developed in  Bosi et al. (2015)  and  Estevan et al. (2013a)  on continuous representability of semiorders and interval orders and modify it in order to apply to the more general case of biorders. This study is done by means of convergence of nets. The use of nets is just a technique to study the continuity by means of convergence conditions and it has already been used in several papers (namely  Bosi et al., 2015  and  Estevan et al., 2013a ) with satisfactory results. Thus, we achieve some necessary conditions that are not strange at all since they can be compared with other well-known concepts as  
                         τ 
                      -continuity of the biorder and the traces or the Hausdorff property.

In order to understand the use of nets, we shall reformulate continuity of a function in terms of convergence of nets or sequences (we will omit the proofs since they can be found in several topology books, i.e.  Dugundji, 1966 ,  Engelking, 1989 ). Before these reformulations, in the following lines we recover some concepts.

A  directed set  
                      
                         I 
                       is a set with a partial order  
                         ⪯ 
                       such that for each pair  
                         α 
                         , 
                         β 
                       of elements of  
                         I 
                      , there exists an element  
                         γ 
                       of  
                         I 
                       having the property that  
                         α 
                         ⪯ 
                         γ 
                       and  
                         β 
                         ⪯ 
                         γ 
                      . A  net  in a topological space  
                         
                            ( 
                            X 
                            , 
                            τ 
                            ) 
                         
                       is a function  
                         f 
                       from a directed set  
                         I 
                       into  
                         X 
                      . If  
                         α 
                         ∈ 
                         I 
                      , we usually denote  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            α 
                            ) 
                         
                       by  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               α 
                            
                         
                      . We denote the net  
                         f 
                       itself by the symbol  
                         
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        α 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            
                               α 
                               ∈ 
                               I 
                            
                         
                      , or merely by  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  α 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       if the index set is understood.

The net  
                         
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        α 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            
                               α 
                               ∈ 
                               I 
                            
                         
                       is said to  converge  to the point  
                         x 
                       of  
                         X 
                       (written  
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  α 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                         → 
                         x 
                      ) if for each neighbourhood  
                         U 
                       of  
                         x 
                      , there exists  
                         α 
                         ∈ 
                         I 
                       such that  
                         α 
                         ⪯ 
                         β 
                       implies  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               β 
                            
                         
                         ∈ 
                         U 
                      . Notice that this definition reduces to  sequences  when  
                         I 
                       is the totally ordered set of the natural numbers.  
                         Lemma 18 
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               f 
                             
                            be a function from  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        X 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            to  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Y 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        Y 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            and  
                            
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            
                            . Then,  
                            
                               f 
                             
                            is continuous on  
                            
                               x 
                             
                            if and only if for every net  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              α 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     α 
                                     ∈ 
                                     I 
                                  
                               
                             
                            in  
                            
                               X 
                             
                            converging to  
                            
                               x 
                            
                            , the net  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              α 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     α 
                                     ∈ 
                                     I 
                                  
                               
                             
                            converges to  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            .

Let  
                            
                               f 
                             
                            be a function from  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        X 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            to  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Y 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        Y 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            and  
                            
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            
                            . Then,  
                            
                               f 
                             
                            is continuous on  
                            
                               x 
                             
                            if and only if for every net  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              α 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     α 
                                     ∈ 
                                     I 
                                  
                               
                             
                            in  
                            
                               X 
                             
                            converging to  
                            
                               x 
                            
                            , the net  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              α 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     α 
                                     ∈ 
                                     I 
                                  
                               
                             
                            converges to  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            .

Since the concept of a net generalizes the notion of a sequence, it also holds true that if a function  
                         f 
                       is continuous on an element  
                         x 
                       of  
                         X 
                      , then for every sequence  
                         
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        n 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            
                               n 
                               ∈ 
                               N 
                            
                         
                       in  
                         X 
                       converging to  
                         x 
                       the sequence  
                         f 
                         
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        n 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            
                               n 
                               ∈ 
                               N 
                            
                         
                       converges to  
                         f 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            ) 
                         
                      . However, the converse implication is false for the general case (see  Dugundji, 1966 ,  Engelking, 1989 ).

Nevertheless, as the following lemma shows, in some cases we can study continuity through sequences instead of through nets.  
                         Lemma 19 
                         
                            Let  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        X 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            and  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Y 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        Y 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            be two first-countable topological spaces. Let  
                            
                               f 
                             
                            be a function from  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        X 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            to  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Y 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        Y 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            and  
                            
                               x 
                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            
                            . Then,  
                            
                               f 
                             
                            is continuous on  
                            
                               x 
                             
                            if and only if for every sequence  
                            
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              n 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     ∈ 
                                     N 
                                  
                               
                             
                            in  
                            
                               X 
                             
                            converging to  
                            
                               x 
                            
                            , the sequence  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              n 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     ∈ 
                                     N 
                                  
                               
                             
                            converges to  
                            
                               f 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  ) 
                               
                            
                            .

Let  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  X 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        X 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            and  
                            
                               
                                  ( 
                                  Y 
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        τ 
                                     
                                     
                                        Y 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                             
                            be two first-countable topological spaces. Let  
                            
                               f

                                  (

                            and

                               ∈ 
                               X 
                            
                            . Then,

                            is continuous on  
                            
                               x

                                        (

                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     ∈ 
                                     N 
                                  
                               
                             
                            in  
                            
                               X 
                             
                            converging to  
                            
                               x

                               f 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              n 
                                           
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     n 
                                     ∈ 
                                     N 
                                  
                               
                             
                            converges to

                                  x 
                                  )
